talk_title: Targeting RAS and RAF in MSS Metastatic Colorectal Cancer

speaker.name: Scott Kopetz
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Department of Gastrointestinal Medical Oncology

MOA_summary: Encouraging BRAF V600E inhibition in MSS colorectal cancer requires concurrent EGFR blockade to prevent feedback re‑activation of MAPK signaling; adding chemotherapy (FOLFOX) further suppresses emergent resistant clones and reduces ctDNA‑detectable resistance mutations, leading to higher response rates and prolonged PFS/OS. Parallel KRAS allele‑specific inhibitors (G12C, G12D) show modest activity alone, which is markedly enhanced when combined with EGFR antibodies, reflecting the same feedback loop. Emerging degraders and pan‑RAS approaches aim to overcome subclonal resistance mechanisms such as MET amplification, KRAS/NRAS secondary mutations, and YAP/TEAD activation.
key_findings: BreakWater first‑line BRAF V600E + EGFR inhibition plus FOLFOX achieved ORR 67% vs 37% with standard chemotherapy (HR for PFS 0.53, HR for OS 0.49).
Median OS doubled from 15.1 months (control) to 30.3 months with EC+FOLFOX in BRAF V600E MSS colorectal cancer.
ctDNA clearance of the BRAF V600E clone at 6 weeks increased from 35% (control) to 45% (EC) and 67% (EC+FOLFOX), correlating with improved outcomes.
Acquired resistance mutations (KRAS, NRAS, MAP2K1, MET amplification) were subclonal and more frequent after EC alone than after EC+FOLFOX.
KRAS G12C inhibitor adagrasib combined with cetuximab yielded ORR 34% and PFS 6.9 months; elacestrant monotherapy gave ORR 47% (up to 62% with EGFR inhibitor).
High baseline cell‑cycle signaling predicts emergence of secondary resistance mutations; low cytolytic score predicts greater benefit from BRAF‑EGFR therapy.
Pre‑clinical data show cross‑resistance between prior chemotherapy and BRAF‑EGFR therapy, supporting first‑line use of the combination.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Secondary KRAS/NRAS/MAP2K1 mutations and MET amplifications emerge at progression, often subclonal (≈1% of tumor cells).
High baseline cell‑cycle transcriptional activity predicts acquisition of resistance mutations.
YAP/TEAD signaling up‑regulation observed in paired biopsies after KRAS inhibition, suggesting a bypass pathway.

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks EGFR‑mediated MAPK re‑activation that limits BRAF inhibitor efficacy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression; ORR 67% with EC+FOLFOX vs 37% control.

combination_strategies.rationale: Suppresses emergent resistant clones and reduces ctDNA‑detectable resistance mutations.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved PFS (12.8 mo vs 7 mo) and OS (30.3 mo vs 15.1 mo).

combination_strategies.rationale: KRAS inhibition may prime tumor microenvironment for immune activation.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Early data suggest increased lymphocyte clustering and immune activation in paired biopsies.

combination_strategies.rationale: Degrader plus EGFR inhibition superior to BRAF‑EGFR combo in pre‑clinical models.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor regression in PDX models.

open_questions: What is the optimal sequencing of BRAF‑EGFR therapy with cytotoxic chemotherapy to minimize cross‑resistance?
Can pan‑RAS or KRAS G12D inhibitors achieve durable responses without EGFR blockade, or is EGFR inhibition always required?
What are the mechanisms driving the indolent biology of post‑BreakWater resistant tumors (PFS2), and can they be therapeutically exploited?
